coredata.dc:title: Recent progress in green and biopolymer based photocatalysts for the abatement of aquatic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Suresh, R.
Rajendran, Saravanan
Hoang, Tuan K.A.
Vo, Dai-Viet N.
Siddiqui, Mohammad Nahid
Cornejo-Ponce, Lorena
coredata.dc:description: Enormous research studies on the abatement of anthropogenic aquatic pollutants including organic dyes, pesticides, cosmetics, antibiotics and inorganic species by using varieties of semiconductor photocatalysts have been reported in recent decades. Besides, many of these photocatalysts suffer in real applications owing to their high production cost and low stability. In many cases, the photocatalysts themselves are being considered as secondary pollutants. To eliminate these drawbacks, the green synthesized photocatalysts and the use of biopolymers as photocatalyst supports are considered in recent years. In this context, recent developments in green synthesized metals, metal oxides, other metal compounds, and carbon based photocatalysts in water purification are critically reviewed. Furthermore, the pivotal role of biopolymers including chitin, chitosan, cellulose, natural gum, hydroxyapatite, alginate in photocatalytic removal of aquatic pollutants is comprehensively reviewed. The presence of functional groups, electron trapping ability, biocompatibility, natural occurrence, and low production cost are the major reasons for using biopolymers in photocatalysis. Finally, the summary and conclusion are presented along with existing challenges in this research area.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enormous research studies on the abatement of anthropogenic aquatic pollutants including organic dyes, pesticides, cosmetics, antibiotics and inorganic species by using varieties of semiconductor photocatalysts have been reported in recent decades. Besides, many of these photocatalysts suffer in real applications owing to their high production cost and low stability. In many cases, the photocatalysts themselves are being considered as secondary pollutants. To eliminate these drawbacks, the green synthesized photocatalysts and the use of biopolymers as photocatalyst supports are considered in recent years. In this context, recent developments in green synthesized metals, metal oxides, other metal compounds, and carbon based photocatalysts in water purification are critically reviewed. Furthermore, the pivotal role of biopolymers including chitin, chitosan, cellulose, natural gum, hydroxyapatite, alginate in photocatalytic removal of aquatic pollutants is comprehensively reviewed. The presence of functional groups, electron trapping ability, biocompatibility, natural occurrence, and low production cost are the major reasons for using biopolymers in photocatalysis. Finally, the summary and conclusion are presented along with existing challenges in this research area.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, the water pollution has become a major global issue. The contamination of water ecosystems is occurred by organic, inorganic and organometallic pollutants, and obviously by pathogens, too (Häder et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2019; Schmeller et al., 2018). Each year, several tons of organic dyes are discharged from textile industries into nearby aquatic ecosystems (Berradi et al., 2019; Ghaly et al., 2014). Organic dyes adversely alter physicochemical characteristics of natural water. For instance, the existence of organic dye in water decreases the sunlight penetration and thus hampers the photosynthesis of aquatic plants (Moorthy et al., 2021). Ultimately it affects aquatic animals (Tkaczyk et al., 2020). Dyes also increase the biological oxygen demand which causes ultimately disruption of aquatic ecosystems (Lellis et al., 2019). It has been determined that the high concentration of indigo dye could reduce the growth of Scenedesmus quadricauda (Chia and Musa, 2014). Methylene blue, a vastly used textile dye shows toxicity towards microalgae (Chia and Musa, 2014). It also decreases the growth of Spirulina platensi and Chlorella vulgaris by suppressing production of chlorophyll (Ma et al., 2020). Besides, organic dye itself shows carcinogenic and mutagenic effects on living things. Malachite green (N-methylated diaminotriphenylmethane) is a best example which shows toxic effect to mammalian cells. It also causes irritation to the gastrointestinal tract in humans, reproductive issues in rabbit and tumours in mice (Ma et al., 2020; Gopinathan et al., 2015). From agriculture and health care sectors, various organic pollutants including cosmetics, pharmaceutical products, microplastics, sweeteners, surfactants, insecticides, fertilizers and pesticides are discharged into natural water ecosystems (Lin et al., 2020; Rosal et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2020). Many of these pollutants are not able to be removed by current water treatment plants (Petrie et al., 2015). The persistence of traces (1–100 ng L−1) of these pollutants is harmful to living organisms (Bueno et al., 2012). For example, 3-benzophenone shows endocrine disruption in water and toxicity to humans (Ghazipura et al., 2017). Metformin, an anti-diabetic molecule could adversely affect human health (Velázquez and Oliván, 2020). On the other hand, occurrence of heavy metals in water resources also causes serious health issues (Egorova and Ananikov, 2017). From anthropogenic sources, heavy metals are entering in water ecosystems and they will not degrade naturally (Ledezma et al., 2021), whereas, they cause bioaccumulation or biomagnification that deleterious effect overall ecosystems (Mathew et al., 2019). For instance, chromium (Cr) occurs as Cr(VI)/Cr(III) ions in water resources. Naturally, Cr(VI) species are a carcinogenic substance (Sharma et al., 2021). Trace amounts of mercury species bring injuries in plants organs and show gastrointestinal, nephro and neurotoxicity (Yang et al., 2020a). Arsenic (As) is also persisted into water bodies due to natural and man-made processes (Herath et al., 2016). It exists as As(V)/As(III) ions. Among them, As(III) ions exhibit higher toxicity than As(V) ions to living things (Ferreira et al., 2019). Organic and inorganic forms of selenium (Se) are also present in natural waters (Kumkrong et al., 2018). The excess level of organic and inorganic selenium was found to be more toxic substance (Naderi et al., 2021). Another important class of aquatic pollutants is organometallic compounds of arsenic, mercury, and tin. For example, the adverse effects of tributyltin in marine organisms were reported. The higher level and lower-level living marine organisms are even affected just by 1 μg L−1 and 1 ng L−1 of tributyltin respectively (Ladislao, 2008). Roxarsone, an organoarsenic compound shows toxic effect due to its degradation in environment (Tang et al., 2020). Thimerosal, an organomercuric compound is utilized as antifungal and bacterial agent in vaccines (Cunha et al., 2020). Nevertheless, thimerosal toxicity has also been reported (Bernard et al., 2001). Moreover, it undergoes natural degradation in aquatic environments to release highly toxic ethyl mercury (Santos et al., 2018). Therefore, direct discharge of above said pollutants contaminated waters into water ecosystems without proper treatment would possibly contaminate both ground and surface waters and threat aquatic living things and humans.

Besides, in 2020 United Nations world water development report, it is reported that 2.2 billion people couldn't get safe drinking water while 4.2 billion people did not have sanitation service (World Water Developmen, 2020). In addition, the lack of safe water and poor hygiene will spread dysentery, cholera, typhoid, diarrhea, etc., and cause a number of deaths worldwide. Therefore, aquatic pollutants must be completely removed before discharge into water ecosystems. Thus, pollutant removal is one of the most predominant goals for researchers who are working on wastewater purification. Moreover, the growing energy demand and pollution is urged to explore low cost and sustainable materials for applications in waste water treatment.

To remove pollutants from wastewater, different treatment techniques such as coagulation/flocculation, reverse osmosis, electrochemical, adsorption, ion exchange, membrane filtration, biological degradation and sonochemical methods have been used (Jiang, 2015; Unal et al., 2020a; dos Santos et al., 2021; Taka et al., 2021; Hansima et al., 2021; Pronk et al., 2019; Hasan et al., 2020; Yasuda, 2021; Zhao et al., 2020). However, these methods have their own drawbacks such as low removal efficiency, requirement of high power, expensive instruments/chemicals and high maintenance costs, complicated procedures and causes of secondary pollution (Hitam and Jalil, 2020). Therefore, low cost and sustainable method with high removal efficiency is required for wastewater purification. In this article, recent progress in photocatalysis for the abatement of organic contaminants is reviewed in detail. In particular, the focus on green synthetic photocatalysts and biopolymer-based composite photocatalysts in the organic, inorganic, and organometallic pollutant removal was given.

In recent years, the application of advanced oxidation processes in wastewater purification has been considered (Giwa et al., 2021). These oxidation processes generate hydroxyl radicals by in-situ chemical reactions. Due to high oxidation power (2.80 V versus standard hydrogen electrode), the hydroxyl radicals could mineralize organic contaminants effectively. By using these processes, various non-biodegradable and stable aquatic pollutants could be removed faster. The most important advantages of these processes are: (i) the pollutants are completely removed or considerably decrease in water via the mineralization process; (ii) no toxic byproducts are produced; and (iii) secondary pollution could be avoided. Among different advanced oxidation processes, photocatalysis is considered to be a promising and sustainable method, since it involves the utilization of solar energy, an abundant renewable energy resource. In this context, semiconductor-based photocatalysis is a preferable and energy-effective technology, since it can be applied for removal of wide varieties aquatic pollutants with freely available solar light along with suitable photocatalyst. The generation of hydroxyl radicals as well as superoxide radicals is possible upon illumination of suitable light energy on photocatalyst suspension containing aquatic pollutants. Solar energy could enhance the sustainability of industry and reduce environmental pollution. It is known that photocatalyst has a vital role in photocatalytic degradation reactions. In order to effectively apply photocatalytic technology in wastewater treatment, scientists have made remarkable efforts to enhance their properties.

The performance of semiconductor and/or metal photocatalysts could be increased by using support materials, since they form facile interactions with contaminants and subsequent transportation of reactive free radicals and degradation. The fundamental requirements of an ideal support material are capacity to make strong interactions with photocatalyst and should have a huge surface area, strong adsorption property and high stability (Khaneghah and Yangjeh, 2020). Generally, conducting polymers and carbon materials including reduced graphene oxide, graphene, etc., are used as photocatalyst support materials (Suresh et al., 2018). Especially, conducting polymers like polyaniline and their derivatives, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene), polypyrrole, polydopamine, polythiophene are commonly used synthetic polymers in wastewater treatment (Kumar et al., 2020a). Due to their physicochemical behaviors, these polymers effectively trap photogenerated electrons and lead greater enhanced photocatalytic degradation efficiencies. However, low solubility, low thermal stability, low specific surface area, high production cost and weak mechanical strength of polymers restrict their practical utility in wastewater treatment. Also, some polymers may afford toxicity to environment and thus cause secondary environmental pollution (Taghizadeh et al., 2020). Hence, to attain a sustainable photocatalytic material, a considerable attention has been given on natural biopolymers including chitin, chitosan, starch, hydroxyapatite, etc., since they are renewable and vastly available in nature (Sarkar et al., 2020). Moreover, they have porous structure and abundant polar functional groups which favors interaction with contaminants. With this background, this review mainly focuses on green synthesized photocatalyst as well as biopolymer based photocatalysts for the treatment of a wide range of aquatic pollutants. Hope, this review will add knowledge on development in sustainable photocatalysts in wastewater treatment.

According to Scopus database, the search results (as on January 12, 2021) with keywords of ‘green photocatalyst’ and ‘biopolymer photocatalyst’, in the title, keywords and abstract of publications are displayed in Fig. 1 . The search results on photocatalytic activity of green photocatalysts related to dye degradation, hydrogen production, and water splitting reaction. To have a deeper focus on water treatment, keywords ‘green photocatalyst + wastewater’ and ‘biopolymer photocatalyst + wastewater’ are used. Obviously, between 1980 and 2021 the number of published articles related to applications of green and biopolymer based photocatalysts in the removal of contaminants shows an increasing trend. Nevertheless, the significant increase is observed in between 2010 and 2020. Due to this enormous interest from researchers to apply green photocatalyst in wastewater treatment, authors have been encouraged to write reviews in this field. Based on the obtained Scopus search results, there are very few reviews on photocatalytic application of green photocatalysts in pollutant removal (Boury and Plumejeau, 2015; Kumar et al., 2020b, 2020c). The significance of these reviews has been compared in Table 1 (Kumar et al., 2020c; Santhoshkumar et al., 2017; Torabi et al., 2020; Saravanan et al., 2021; Mondal and Anweshan, 2020; Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2021; Bee and Hamid, 2020; Ibrahim et al., 2020; Adnan et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2020; Mohan et al., 2020). From Table 1, the following observations have been made: (i) some reviews have only focused on synthesis of green photocatalyst and biopolymers. For examples, in 2017, Santhoshkumar et al. (2017), have written a critical review on green synthesis of gold nanoparticles. Mohan et al. (2020) have focused extraction of chitin and chitosan from insects in their review article; (ii) certain review mainly focused on adsorption property of green photocatalyst. Bee and Hamid (2020) have published review on applications of hydroxyapatite based nanomaterials, in which pollutant removal by adsorption was focused; and (iii) some reviews exclusively written on specific green photocatalysts. For instance, Torabi et al. (2020) and Mondal et al. (Mondal and Anweshan, 2020) have focused on gold, Ag2O and iron based photocatalysts, synthesized by green approach. With this situation, this review gives importance to recent developments on both green synthesized metals, meal oxides, metal compounds, carbon compounds and biopolymer-based photocatalysts, which have been systematically classified and reviewed critically. As the final point, we outline the advantages and disadvantages of green and biopolymer-based photocatalyst and also provide possible solutions and challenges in this interesting research area.

As mentioned earlier, generally semiconductors along with appropriate light energy can be used for destroying aquatic contaminants. Depending upon the phase of photocatalyst and reactants, photocatalysis is differentiated as homogenous and heterogeneous photocatalysis.

According to IUPAC definition (Compendium of Chem, 1997), homogeneous catalysis is the one in which only one phase is involved, i.e., reactants and catalysts are in the same phase. For example, considering the degradation of an aqueous solution of paracetamol by using Fenton and photo Fenton reactions, the solution contains mixture of iron(II) salt and H2O2 produces hydroxyl radicals (Jamil et al., 2017). As hydroxyl radicals have strong oxidizing capacity, they attack paracetamol molecules until these molecules degrade into simple inorganic species. In this process, Fenton reagent and paracetamol are in the aqueous phase. Hence, this can be termed as homogenous photo-catalysis. The Fenton process could further accelerated by irradiation of ultraviolet-C (100 < λ < 280 nm) which is called photo Fenton reaction (pH = 3; irradiation time = 50 min). Apart from paracetamol, bisphenol A and dibutyl phthalate are also completely removed by this process.
(1) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3+ + HO− + HO•

Unlike Fenton reagent, there is a soluble inorganic semiconductor photocatalyst that can be used in water treatment and water splitting reaction (Amirian et al., 2018; Li et al., 2017). The best example is the sodium molybdate (Na2MoO4) (Li et al., 2017). In fact, Na2MoO4 is soluble in water owing to its ionization ability. It results in the destruction of crystalline structure and the loss of band structures. However, in over saturated solution, precipitates of Na2MoO4 remain crystalline which absorbs light and produces photo-induced charge carriers. Therefore undissolved soluble semiconductors can be used as photocatalysts. For example, saturated solution of (1.05 g in 10 mL of water) Na2MoO4 photocatalyst is used in the overall water splitting reaction under the light created from light-emitting diode bulbs (λ = 365 nm). The result suggests that undissolved Na2MoO4 photocatalyst is highly stable for more than two weeks (Li et al., 2017). Perhaps there are some disadvantages in homogenous photo-catalysis. They are (i) homogenous photocatalysts cannot be recovered and reusable. (ii) In case of Fenton process, acidic pH needs to be maintained. (iii) More amount of sludge will be generated. (iii) Secondary pollutant will be generated. On the other hand, heterogeneous photo-catalysis provides a possible solution for these disadvantages (Antonopoulou et al., 2021).

According to IPUAC definition, the reaction occurs at or near an interface between two different phases is termed as heterogeneous catalysis. In this reaction, both catalyst and reactants are in different phases. For example, the insoluble ZnO is dispersed in water containing roxarsone suspension, and the system is illuminated with ultraviolet irradiation. The roxarsone molecule undergoes decomposition at the surface of ZnO solid particles (Acuña et al., 2017). Apart from wastewater purification, heterogeneous photocatalysis is also considered in organic transformation (Han and Sun, 2021) and hydrogen generation (Saravanan et al., 2018a), since it has the following advantages: (i) it needs only ambient environmental condition, (ii) the recovery of photocatalysts is possible; (iii) available dissolved oxygen in water can be directly used for photocatalytic degradation reactions; (iv) complete mineralization of organic pollutants is possible. In heterogeneous photo-catalysis, under the irradiation of the light source, photoinduced reaction occurs on the surface of the catalyst. After 1970, the photocatalytic behaviour of TiO2 towards degradation of various pollutants was studied (Fujishima and Honda, 1972). Alternatively, the photocatalytic oxidation behaviour of ZnO was first reported in the year of 1982 (Pichat et al., 1982). Later on, significant attention has been given on photocatalytic properties of ZnO in water treatment. However, both TiO2 and ZnO have a large band gap (~3.3 eV) which can harvest only 5% of ultraviolet region in solar light (Cordero et al., 2016). After that, visible active photocatalysts have been developed, these catalysts can utilize 45% of the visible region in the solar spectrum. However, both ultraviolet and visible active photocatalysts suffer in the real wastewater treatment application due to their low photocatalytic efficiency. It is due to the fast recombination (few ps ~ ns) of photogenerated electrons and holes that occur due to the extremely strong electrostatic force of attraction (Xu et al., 2018). Photo-generated charges are responsible for photo-oxidation and reduction reaction that occur at the photocatalyst surface. When the concentration of photocharges decreases, photo redox reaction rate also decreases. Furthermore, broad sunlight harvesting capacity and strong redox ability are primary criteria for an ideal photocatalyst (Xu et al., 2020). To have strong redox ability, a single photocatalyst should have high conduction band and low valence band position – i.e., wide band gap. However, wide-band gap material could absorb only 5% of solar energy. According to thermodynamics, the difference between conduction and valence band potential determines feasibility of reaction to occur. To exhibit broad solar light harvesting efficiency, a single photocatalyst should have low conduction band and shallow valence band positions. Hence, single photocatalyst with broad light absorption and strong redox ability could not be achieved simultaneously. Hence, to improve the photocatalytic performance, strategies such as tuning of morphology (Pandiyarajan et al., 2017) and size (Zhou et al., 2019), metal or nonmetal doping (Patnaik et al., 2021; Suresh et al., 2017a), creating surface defects (Saravanan et al., 2018b) and construction of heterogeneous structures or nanocomposites (Suresh et al., 2017b) have been reported. But, in this review, we restrict only green and biopolymer based heterostructure photocatalysts which have had studied in water treatment. The heterostructure photocatalysts are synthesized by combining two or more photocatalytic materials with appropriate ratios. Depending on the number of components, they are termed as binary, ternary and so on. Herein photogeneration of charges by binary semiconductor nanocomposite photocatalysts are discussed (Fig. 2 ).
(i)
Type-I photocatalyst: In type - I photocatalyst, the positions of valence and conduction band of semiconductor - A should be more positive and negative than those of semiconductor - B. In presence of appropriate light source, photogenerated electrons and holes of semiconductor - A are simultaneously shifted to semiconductor – B (Zhang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, the probability of recombination of electron-hole pair within the same semiconductor surface is high (Li et al., 2020).
(ii)
Type-II photocatalyst: In this type of photocatalyst, photoinduced electron - hole pairs are generated within both semiconductor A and B under light illumination. Then, photogenerated electrons are shifted from semiconductor – A to semiconductor - B, while photogenerated holes transfer to the reverse direction. It causes accumulation of electrons on semiconductors – B while semiconductor – A acquires holes. It provides sites for oxidation and reduction reactions at semiconductor – A and B respectively. Hence, the photogenerated charges are spatially separated (Jia et al., 2017).
(iii)
Z-scheme: In order to reduce the recombination rate and keep strong redox ability, Z-scheme photocatalysts were developed. As Type – I and II, this system consists of two semiconductors with staggered band structures. Unlike type-I and II, in Z-scheme photocatalyst, holes in semiconductor - A react with electron donors to form electron acceptors. Then the excited electrons in the conduction band of semiconductor - B reacts with an electron acceptor, provoking electron donor. After that, the reserved excited electrons in the conduction band of the semiconductor – A and holes in the valence band of semiconductor - B participate in the photo reduction and oxidation reactions, respectively (Kumar et al., 2021).
(iv)
S-scheme photocatalyst: Generally, according to the band structures, photocatalysts are classified as reduction photocatalysts and oxidation photocatalysts (Fu et al., 2019). Reduction photocatalyst possess high conduction band position and thus photogenerated electrons are effective, while holes are useless. An additional sacrificial agent is also required to remove holes. In case of oxidation photocatalyst, photogenerated holes are the main species to bring degradation reactions, while electrons are unproductive. An S-scheme photocatalyst contains both reduction and oxidation photocatalysts. The powerful electrons and holes are reserved in the conduction band of reduction photocatalyst and valence band of oxidation photocatalyst, respectively.

From the above, it should be understood that the selection of appropriate components for fabrication of nanocomposite photocatalysts is an essential step. Nonetheless, the disadvantages such as low dispersion in water, fast recombination of electron-hole pairs, mass transfer limitation, and toxicity make it unsuitable for practical applications in water treatment plants (Singh et al., 2019; Iervolino et al., 2020). Hence, researchers are working on sustainable photocatalysts for wastewater treatment. In particular, green synthesized and biopolymer based nanocomposites has emerged as best photocatalysts due to their interesting features which are discussed in successive sections.

Extensive investigations have been performed for fabrication of photocatalysts to tune their optical, catalytic and magnetic properties. Consequently, synthesis methods like solvothermal, sol-gel method, hydrothermal, polyol synthesis, solution combustion, microemulsion, thermal decomposition, sonochemical, microwave irradiation, ball-milling, spray pyrolysis, laser ablation methods, chemical vapour deposition, electrochemical and photochemical method were reported. However, many of these synthesis methods require high temperature and pressure, long reaction time, costly instruments & accessories, many toxic and expensive chemicals (Kirankumar and Sumathi, 2020). Apart from low yield, some of these methods may also generate toxic by-products. For example, nanostructured metal oxides are generally synthesized in basic medium. Sodium hydroxide, an extremely corrosive chemical is usually used to maintain basic pH for nanoparticles synthesis. After completion of reaction, supernatant is directly discarded into wastewater. Upon contact with metals, sodium hydroxide can even produce flammable hydrogen (Phung et al., 2018). However, green synthesis is evolved into a significant offshoot of nanotechnology. In this contest, various parts of plant-derived extracts (leaf, root, flower, seed and latex), microorganisms (bacteria, fungi, algae, and virus) and biological material (honey, starch, and ascorbic acid) are used for the synthesis of semiconductor and plasmonic photocatalysts (Dhandapani et al., 2020; Motazedi et al., 2020) (Fig. 3 ). The use of plant-derived extracts for preparation of photocatalyst is having a number of advantages when compared to microbial processes (bacteria and fungi), because microbes will contaminate photocatalysts (Ahmed et al., 2016). The plant-mediated syntheses are rendering the following potential advantages.
(i)
Reducing/precipitating agent

Plant extract contains flavonoids, tannins, anthraquinone, anthocyanins, phytosterols, alkaloids, phlobatannins, terpenoids, steroids, saponins, glycosides, and phenol which can donate hydrogen and chelate with metal ions easily. For example, pomegranate (P. granatum) seed extract contains p-hydroxy benzoic acid, ellagic acid, retusin (isoflavone), kaempferol-3-O-sophoroside, catechin, apigenin, 3-deoxyflavonoids, methyl gallate, syringic acid, phloretin, magnolol, flavogallonic acid dilactone, ferulic acid, gallic acid, citric acid, vanillic acid, pinocembrin, and ellagic acid derivatives (Bibi et al., 2019). These organic compounds could easily reduce silver(I), copper(II) and gold(III) ions into their metallic state. Also, they can form complex with divalent and monovalent metal ions to form precipitates that will be calcined to get metal oxide nanostructures.
(ii)
Cheaper and eco-friendly method

Plants are naturally occurring resources and can also be cultivated. Unlike chemical reducing agents and precipitating agents, production cost of plant extracts is less. Also, extracts are obtained easily obtained from plants and thus they are safe.
(iii)
Stable nanoparticles

The nanoparticles obtained from plant mediated synthesis are generally stable due to their stabilization by natural organic molecules presents in these extracts.
(iv)
Good activity

When compared to photocatalyst, synthesized by other methods, green synthesized photocatalysts show good performances. The organic content on the surface of photocatalysts will ultimately favour interaction with pollutant molecules which are essential criteria for best photocatalyst.
(v)
Antimicrobial properties

Green synthesized photocatalyst showed good antimicrobial activity against common pathogens (Pachaiappan et al., 2021). It is added advantage for a best photocatalyst which will be used in water treatment.

Beside Au and Ag, Cu nanoparticles are also synthesized by using Jatropha curcas leaf extract (Ghosh et al., 2020). The Jatropha curcas contains flavonoids, tannins, glycosides, and alkaloids which act as reducing and stabilizing agents (stable up to six months) for Cu nanoparticles. The synthesized Cu nanoparticles (10 mg) exhibit good photocatalytic activity against methylene blue (pH = 7, 10 mg L−1) degradation in presence of sunlight (85 min). On the other hand, Celastrus paniculatus leaf extract assisted synthesized Cu nanoparticles (10 mg) show 90% of photocatalytic degradation efficiency of methylene blue (pH = 9) under (flux = 1100 lx) sunlight (Mali et al., 2020). Also, cubic and rod-like Fe nanoparticles are prepared by using the Actinidia chinensis fruit extract (Ahmed et al., 2020a). The synthesized Fe nanoparticles show good photocatalytic efficiency (93.7%, 42 h) towards degradation of alizarin yellow R dye under sunlight irradiation. Besides the superior photocatalytic performances of metal nanoparticles, their practical application is questionable. Especially silver and gold sources are highly expensive due to their least abundance in the earth crust. In case of iron (Fe) nanoparticles, stability is a major issue, since it undergoes corrosion during photocatalysis. Obviously, recovery is another major concern, since metal nanoparticles are stable colloids (Unal et al., 2020b).

Nanostructured metal oxides including ZnO, TiO2, SnO2, CuO, Fe2O3, etc., have been synthesized by using green chemistry approach. The list of metal oxides synthesized by green synthesis method and their photocatalytic applications are given in Table 2 (Ngoepe et al., 2020; Bibi et al., 2017; Vidya et al., 2016; Ramar et al., 2019; Rafique et al., 2020; Rashmi et al., 2020; Arularasu et al., 2018; Honarmand et al., 2019; Ezhilarasi et al., 2020; Surendra and Mohana Roopan, 2016; Shinde et al., 2018; Ayodhya and Veerabhadram, 2017; Ghanbari et al., 2017; Ajabshir et al., 2018a; Ajabshir et al., 2018b; Garg et al., 2018; Yadav et al., 2019; Fardood et al., 2020; Shanker et al., 2017; Rahnamaeiyan and Talebi, 2016; Talebi et al., 2016; Rahni and Kakhki, 1002).

Unlike metal nanoparticle synthesis, organic compounds in plant extract will form complex with metal ions and precipitated in aqueous solution. Obviously additional heat energy is also needed. The as-formed precipitate will be further annealed to obtain metal oxide nanoparticles. Among many other metal oxides, relatively the number of publications on the green synthesis of ZnO nanoparticles with photocatalytic activity is high. It was synthesized by using various plant extracts. For example, ZnO nanoparticles could be prepared by using Artocarpus heterophyllus (jackfruit) leaf extract (Vidya et al., 2016). The phytochemicals present in this extract act as precipitating as well as stabilizing agents. ZnO nanoparticles show greater than 80% of photocatalytic degradation efficiency within an hour of light irradiation against rose Bengal dye. The same research group also used Artocarpus heterophyllus extract for the preparation of ZnO nanoparticles (Vidya et al., 2017). This leaf extract contains amines, amides, quinines and ketones which act as a precipitating agent. The prepared photocatalyst (0.24 g L−1) exhibits 90% efficiency towards photodegradation of 20 mg L−1 congo red aqueous solution within 1 h of ultraviolet illumination (30 W; λ = 254 nm; flux = 7.9 mW cm−2). Anil Kumar et al. have synthesized ZnO nanoparticles by using zinc nitrate as the zinc source and Ruta chalepensis leaf extract and NaOH as precipitating agents (Anil Kumar et al., 2019). The reaction time and calcination temperature are 1 h and 450 °C, respectively. R. chalepensis extract contains polyphenols, terpenoids, flavonoids, triterpenes and carotenoids. The functional groups of these bioactive compounds could form a complex with Zn(II) ions, and enable the growth of (green method) g-ZnO nanoparticles.
(2) Zn(NO3)2•6H2O + Plant extract + 2NaOH → ZnO + 3NaNO3 + By-products

The (chemical method) c-ZnO nanoparticles are also prepared without using R. chalepensis leaf extract. The prepared ZnO nanoparticles are used as photocatalyst (~60 mg) for decolorization of methylene blue, indigo carmine and malachite green dyes (250 mL, 20 mg L−1) under ultraviolet irradiation. When compared to c-ZnO, g-ZnO showed good photocatalytic performances due to reduction in the band gap and favours dye adsorption by π-π interaction. The quite interesting phenomenon is reduced band gap of ZnO particles by plant extracts. But only ultraviolet light is used to determine its photocatalytic activity in this work. Next to ZnO, the most widely used TiO2 nanostructures are also synthesized with aid of various plant extracts (Saikumari et al., 2019; Singh et al., 2020). Ngoepe et al. have used Monsonia burkeana plant extract and titanium(IV) fluoride (TiF4) as precipitating agent and the titanium source, respectively, for synthesis of TiO2 nanospheres (Ngoepe et al., 2020). This biosynthesized TiO2 nanosphere is applied as the photocatalyst for the degradation of methylene blue under ultraviolet irradiation. Around 85.5% of methylene blue could be successfully removed. In addition, TiO2 exhibits antibacterial activity against Eschericia coli. In another research work, Ag2O nanoparticles are synthesized by using Centella asiatica leaves (Rafique et al., 2020). The prepared Ag2O efficiently decolorizes acid orange 8 under ultraviolet irradiation for 150 min. During irradiation, electron-hole pairs are created within the Ag2O structure. Owing to the presence of half-filled d-orbitals in Ag+ ions, excited electrons are trapped in the conduction band and form Ag0. This process slows down the recombination of electrons with holes.
(3) Ag2O + hν → Ag2O (e− + h+)
(4) Ag+ + e− → Ag0

Then the electron is absorbed by O2 molecules to form O2 •− which gets converted to active OH• radicals.
(5) Ag0 + O2 → Ag+ + O2 •−

Even though significant efforts have been paid for green synthesis of metal oxides as photocatalysts, major issues such as photostability and understandings in flat band potential values are yet to be demonstrated. Furthermore, the capped organic molecules lose their own identity during annealing step, since they may be converted into carbon (Jayaprakash et al., 2020). Excessive carbon might also reduce photocatalytic performance of metal oxides (He et al., 2021). Hence, ratio between metal source and plant extracts should be optimized.

Apart from metal oxides, green synthesis method has also been applied to prepare metal sulphides, chromate, vanadate, aluminate, etc. (Table 2). Ayodhya et al. (2017) have prepared CdS nanoparticles by using Calotropis gigantean leaf extract assisted by ultrasonication for 1 h. The abundant hydroxyl groups in leaf extract could easily make a complex with cadmium ions which further reacts with added sulphide to form CdS nanoparticles. This photocatalyst is utilized for degradation of methylene blue and eosin Y under sun light irradiation. Within 60 min of irradiation time, CdS shows 85.5% and 91.1% degradation efficiency towards methylene blue and eosin Y respectively. The observed antimicrobial activity of CdS against E. coli, B. thuringiensis, S. aureus, A. niger, P. aeruginosa and C. albicans is an added advantage for this photocatalyst as it may use for deactivation of pathogens. Shanker et al. have synthesized nanostructured iron hexacyanoferrate using sapindus mukorossi extract (Shanker et al., 2017). The photocatalytic degradation of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons was performed using iron hexacyanoferrate/sun light system. Under optimized experimental conditions, 90% degradation efficiency was achieved.

The carbon based photocatalysts are also synthesized by green synthesis methods (Das et al., 2019). For example, carbon quantum dots have been synthesized from various natural sources such as tamarind, cabbage, papaya, banana, orange, lime and ginger (Zaib et al., 2020; Duarah and Karak, 2017). Biosynthesized carbon quantum dots show unique optical behaviour and also show good photocatalytic activity towards selected pollutants. Recently, Ramar et al. (2018) have synthesized carbon quantum dots by Citrus maxima extract. The prepared photocatalyst effectively degrades aqueous solution of methylene blue in presence of solar illumination, because carbon quantum dots act as an electron donor and acceptor which results effective separation of charges. The graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has been synthesized from starch (Iqbal et al., 2017). Addition of starch during calcination of melamine significantly enhances the porosity. This porous g-C3N4 shows exceptional photocatalytic activity towards degradation of phenol and rhodamine B. Porous g-C3N4 has enhanced photocatalytic performance owing to its greater specific surface area (67 m2 g−1) and improved mass transfer ability.

In literature review, there are some reports in which composite photocatalysts were synthesized by green approach (Ruan et al., 2019; Kasturi et al., 2020; Mureseanu et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016). For example, NiO/MgO nanocomposite has been synthesized by using peel extract of Punica granatum. Within 10 min of visible light exposure, NiO/MgO shows 87 and 73% degradation efficiency of methylene blue and methyl orange, respectively (Fuku et al., 2018). Jha et al. (2019) have reported Ag/CdO nanocomposite photocatalyst, synthesized by using citrus lemon leaves for photocatalytic decomposition of rhodamine B and methylene blue in water. When the catalyst is exposed to visible light irradiation, electrons are excited to the conduction band of CdO. Then photogenerated electrons are transferred to silver nanoparticles which are absorbed by oxygen molecules to produce reactive anionic superoxide radicals. Afterwards, the generated superoxide anionic radicals interact with surface water molecules to form hydroxyl radicals. The water molecule traps holes from CdO and form hydroxyl radicals. The reactive oxygen species leads the degradation of dye molecules.
(6) CdO + hν → CdO(e− + h+)
(7) CdO(h+ + e−) + Ag → CdO (h+) + Ag(e−)
(8) Ag(e−) + O2 → Ag + O2 •−
(9) CdO(h+) + H2O → CdO + HO• + H+

Yadav et al. have prepared bismuth oxyiodide by using Azadiracha indica leaf extract (Yadav et al., 2019). The synthesized bismuth oxyiodide is embedded in carbon black with different weight ratio. This composite shows 94.1% and 91.8% degradation efficiency against ampicillin and amoxicillin, respectively, after 300 min of reaction. Green synthesized photocatalysts prove high potential for water purification. Nonetheless, the following few drawbacks limit their commercial viability. (a) Optimization of reaction parameters might be difficult, since the concentration and nature of organic contents in plant's extract will vary with climatic seasons. These parameters ultimately affect morphology and size of nanomaterials which alters their photocatalytic behaviors. (b) Photocatalysts with high purity need to obtain. In general, organic compounds in plant extract will surface adsorbed on the photocatalyst. Upon synthesis with calcination step, organic compounds will also undergo decomposition to leave carbon residues. Complete removal of organic compounds or carbon residues from photocatalyst is difficult. (c) Most of the literatures concentrate synthesis of single metal or metal compounds with their photocatalytic performances. But it is well known that composite photocatalyst exhibits better photocatalytic activity. Hence, clear studies for the preparation of composite photocatalysts are required. (d) Photocatalyst prepared by using microbes may show toxicity. Moreover, maintaining of microbial growth parameters for large scale is tedious and thus it will raise production cost of photocatalyst. (e) There are difficulties in recycling, and recovery of photocatalysts especially in case of plasmonic photocatalysts. To eliminate these shortcomings, suitable stabilizing matrix can be used. Biopolymers are excellent stabilizing matrix for photocatalysts as they have advantages such as abundance, availability, and low cost.

Biopolymers are generated by living organisms. Based on the monomeric structure, these natural polymers are classified as polysaccharides, polypeptides, and polynucleotides. Among others, generally, polysaccharides are widely used biopolymers along with nanoparticles for various applications (Yang et al., 2020b, 2021a; Zheng et al., 2020; Meneguin et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020). Biopolymers are generally extracted from natural sources. Fig. 5 denotes some important biopolymers with their natural sources including insects, green algae, mammalian bone and fungus (Khayrova et al., 2021). After cleaning process, natural sources were subjected to multiple steps to obtain targeted biopolymer. For instance, to extract chitin, demineralisation procedure (using formic acid) followed deproteinisation (NaOH solution) is applied to Hermetia illucens (Hahn et al., 2020). Organic functionalization is also used to obtain derivative of biopolymers. For example, chitosan is prepared by deacetylation of chitin biopolymer (Hahn et al., 2020). In order to obtain hydroxyapatite, suitable natural sources are collected and pretreated initially. Pretreatment involves frequent washing or boiling with water or combination of both washing and boiling with non-aqueous solvents like acetone. Then, pretreated natural source is subjected to calcination, hydrolysis or hydrothermal treatment. For example, hydroxyapatite can be obtained by calcination of fish bone at 1200 °C (Pal et al., 2017). In this context, recently biopolymers such as alginate, collagen pectin, natural gum, chitin, cellulose, and chitosan have been used as part of photocatalytic materials for decomposition of organic pollutants in water due to the following reasons.
(i)
Abundance

Biopolymers can be extracted from agricultural nonfood crops and exoskeletons of some crustaceans. For example, chitin can be derived from crab, shells of shrimp, and lobster (Knidri et al., 2018). Cellulose, a water-insoluble fibrous polymer is obtained from bananas, sugarcane, wood, and wheat straws (Ganguly et al., 2020). Biopolymers can also be derived by chemical modification of directly derived biopolymers. Chitosan is the best example. It is obtained by N-deacetylation of chitin polymers (Galed et al., 2005). By using wet-spinning method, collagen is extracted from trimming wastes in leather industry (Dixit et al., 2015).
(ii)
Nontoxic

The naturally occurring or derived biopolymers are nontoxic to living things and environment.
(iii)
Functionality

Biopolymers have vast functionality that can be used to bind pollutants through chemical bonds.
(iv)
Good support

These biopolymers are having much attention, since they increase the active surface area and afford strength to photocatalyst. In addition, biopolymers also effectively absorb electrons from the conduction band of photocatalyst and thus inhibit the recombination rate effectively.
(vi)
Photocatalyst

Unlike organic biopolymers, naturally occurring inorganic materials like hydroxyapatite also performs as photocatalyst. There are many publications on photocatalytic behaviours of hydroxyapatite which are described later.

Chitin is a colourless, hard and one of abundant natural polysaccharides. It can be derived from the exoskeleton and in the internal structure of invertebrates. It is biodegradable, biocompatibility and low-cost material. It also possesses multi-coordination sites and has been considered for synthesis of functional hybrid materials (Sarode et al., 2019). For example, Nwankwo et al. have synthesized a rutile TiO2/chitin nanohybrid and demonstrated its photocatalytic activity using methylene blue. The rate constant of the photocatalytic removal of methylene blue is found to be 3.73 ± 0.03 × 10−3 min−1 in 75 min (8 mg TiO2) light illumination time (Nwankwo et al., 2019). On the other hand, chitin whisker containing Ag3PO4 composites were synthesized by chemical deposition method and used them for degradation of rhodamine B dye in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation (Zhang and Liu, 2017). Under the optimized conditions, chitin/Ag3PO4(1:3) could completely degrade rhodamine B dye within 15 min light irradiation. It was found that chitin acts as electron transfer channel which favours the rapid transfer of the photo generated electrons to Ag3PO4 particle's surface. Further, it also provides higher dispersion ability and better stability for the Ag3PO4 photocatalyst. A Cu2O/graphene oxide/chitin ternary composite photocatalyst was also reported for decomposition of aqueous solution of methyl orange (Wang et al., 2015). The chitin/graphene oxide film scatters excited electrons and decrease recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. It further improves the photodegradation efficiency. Apart from metal-based photocatalyst, metal-free photocatalyst was composited with chitin. For example, He et al. have recently prepared chitin-derived carbon/g-C3N4 heterojunction with (32.6 m2 g−1) surface area. This composite shows higher degradation rate constant (8.1 × 10−3 min−1) and efficiency (93.7%) toward photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B dye (He et al., 2020).

Starch is an edible, abundant, cheap, renewable and biodegradable biopolymer (Ojogbo et al., 2020). There are some reports on improvement of photocatalytic performance of semiconductor material by addition of starch (Xue et al., 2020; Xie et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2018). Recently, it has been determined that the addition of starch to α-FeOOH (akageneite)/β-FeOOH (goethite) composite significantly altered their shape, band structure and improved the hydrophilicity (Pal et al., 2020). This ternary nanocomposite photocatalyst shows greater degradation of p-nitrophenol and methyl orange under visible light conditions. Yun et al. (2018)ave synthesized starch/polyvinylalcohol/ZnS nanocomposite for degradation of bisphenol A and methyl orange in presence of ultraviolet light illumination (18 W; 5 Wm-2).

Cellulose is a polysaccharide that comprises a linear chain of several hundreds to many thousands of D-glucose monomers. It has numerous hydroxyl groups that can coordinate with metal cations (Peng et al., 2020). Therefore, metal oxide can be uniformly dispersed on cellulose matrix. Recently, cellulose has been extracted from waste peels of pomegranate, bananas, lime and orange fruits and used as support materials for SnS2 nanoparticles (Tavker and Sharma, 2020). Cellulose/SnS2 photocatalyst has been used as a catalyst in the photodegradation of rhodamine B. About 500 mg cellulose loaded SnS2 shows maximum degradation rate (~0.087 min−1) and photocatalytic efficiency (91%) than that of pure SnS2 photocatalyst. The observed higher photocatalytic performance is due to photogenerated electron trapping ability of cellulose, i.e., surface oxygen of cellulose converts into superoxide radicals which further accelerated radical chain reaction. So, cellulose effectively separates photogenerated electron-hole pair and achieving enhanced photocatalytic activity than pure SnS2 photocatalyst. Kokilavani et al. (2020) have synthesized Ag/Cu-cellulose nanocomposite (0.2 mg) and used as visible light active photocatalyst for removal of methylene blue (20 mL; 25 mg L−1). Carboxymethyl cellulose, a derivative of cellulose has biocompatibility, availability and high adsorption ability. It was coupled with ZnFe2O4 to improve the photocatalytic performance of pure ZnFe2O4 (Malakootian et al., 2019). At pH = 7, 0.3 g of ZnFe2O4/carboxymethyl cellulose composite photocatalyst shows 87% removal efficiency towards simulated ciprofloxacin aqueous solution (5 mg L−1). In case of real contaminated samples, 79% removal efficiency was obtained.

Particularly, metal oxides or carbon-based materials have been incorporated within hydroxyapatite. For instance, hydroxyapatite/dendritic α-Fe2O3 nanocomposite with a mesoporous structure shows almost 100% degradation efficiency towards methyl violet dye solution (Bharath and Ponpandian, 2015). Hydroxyapatite/TiO2 nanocomposite was prepared and applied as ultraviolet driven photocatalyst for the degradation of fluoxetine and diclofenac (2 mg L−1) (Brazon et al., 2016). This (4 g L−1) photocatalyst shows 100% and 92% photocatalytic efficiency for fluoxetine and diclofenac respectively. Mondal et al. (2017) have employed microwave-assisted hydrothermal process for preparation of hydroxyapatite/Au nanocomposite. The 0.055 wt% Au loaded hydroxyapatite exhibits methylene blue removal efficiency of 32.5% after 9 h of visible light illumination. The reasons for observed greater removal efficiency are as follows: (a) generation of oxygen vacancies during the calcination step of hydroxyapatite synthesis, (b) Au nanoparticles act as radical initiator through the generation of hot electrons and (c) this composite could also attract electrons from the methylene blue that leads oxidation of organic moieties. On the other hand, hydroxyapatite also coupled with non-metal compounds such as carbon nitride, reduced graphene oxide phosphorus, etc. For example, Xu et al. (2019) have recently reported hydroxyapatite/g-C3N4, for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline under visible light exposure. Notably, 100 mg of 1.5 wt% g-C3N4 loaded hydroxyapatite composite shows nearly 100% removal efficiency towards degradation of tetracycline in aqueous solution within 15 min. Due to its abundance, metal free and high stability, Zou et al. (2020b) have used red phosphorous to make composites with hydroxyapatite. The 5 wt% red phosphorus loaded hydroxyapatite shows nearly complete degradation within 10, 20 and 30 min of visible light irradiation for tetracycline, rifampicin, and levofloxacin, respectively. Apart from binary composites, ternary composites are also reported. For example, Ag/Ag3PO4/hydroxyapatite photocatalyst has been reported by Piccririllo et al. (Piccirillo et al., 2015). This photocatalyst showed 60% degradation efficiency under visible light illumination while in ultraviolet irradiation, 90% efficiency is achieved after 7 h of reaction. But Chang et al. (2017) achieved 98% methylene blue degradation efficiency within 20 min of visible light irradiation by using N-doped carbon dots/Ag3PO4/hydroxyapatite composite. Very recently, TiO2/SiO2/Al2O3/hydroxyapatite quaternary composite photocatalyst was reported for degradation of rhodamine B dye solution. In this research work, about 98% efficiency was obtained within 90 min of irradiation time (Alorku et al., 2020).

Natural gum is a kind of polysaccharides. It has good antimicrobial properties and binding ability. According to the source of origin, natural gums are classified into four types. They are microbial gum, seed gum, plant exudate gum, and marine gum (Abu Elella et al., 2021). Natural gum based nanocomposites have been used in the removal of organic pollutants from water. For example, elemental iron nanoparticles containing guar gum composites were synthesized and used as photocatalyst for photodegradation reaction of methyl violet dye in presence of solar illumination (Sharma et al., 2019). Guar gum was also added to increase the photocatalytic performance of Al2O3 powder (Pathania et al., 2016). When compared to pure Al2O3, a poor photocatalyst, guar gum added Al2O3 shows 90% of photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards malachite green degradation. The xanthan gum/TiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized and utilized for photodegradation of methyl orange under visible light (Inamuddin, 2019). Owing to the large surface area and visible light harvesting property, this composite photocatalyst shows 74% of degradation efficiency. However, in addition to 1.0 mL of H2O2, it shows 89% methyl orange degradation. In another study, TiO2/arabic gum and TiO2/karaya gum have been used for photocatalytic degradation of 100 mL of 12.0 × 10−6 M methylene blue aqueous solution for 150 min visible light irradiation (Araujo et al., 2020). The addition of these natural gums reduces the band gap of TiO2 from 3.2 eV to 2.9 eV. About 0.5 g L−1 of TiO2/arabic gum and TiO2/karaya gum could effectively decolorize 95% and 90% of methylene blue respectively.

Alginate is one of the important polysaccharides which consist of linear binary copolymers of α-D-mannuronic acid and β-Lguluronic acid (Ramdhan et al., 2020). It is present in brown seaweed. It is abundant and can be easily extracted from its natural sources with low cost. It can form hydrogels with divalent cations that renders the possibility to fabricate hydrogel based photocatalysts (Su et al., 2019). Alginate has been used as support for photocatalytic material due to its high transparency and adsorption capacity. Recently (Zhang et al., 2019), Fe(III)-sodium alginate beads have been fabricated and used for the removal of co-present Cr(VI)/As(III) ions which commonly present in acid wastewater. In fact, the redox reaction between Cr(VI) and As(III) is slow, though this reaction is thermodynamically favourable. However, in presence of Fe(III)-sodium alginate/simulated sunlight irradiation, 100% redox conversion of Cr(VI)/As(III) into Cr(III)/As(V) could be observed within 150 min. The reason for photo-reduction of Cr(VI) is due to the generation of Fe(II) ions and carbonate free radicals. The in situ generated Fe(II) ions reduce Cr(VI) into Cr(III) efficiently.
(10) Fe(III)- sodium alginate + hv → Fe(II) + •CO2 −alginate
(11) 3Fe(II) + Cr(VI) → 3Fe(III) + Cr(III)

Under light exposure, Fe(III)-sodium alginate also generates carbon centred free radicals. According to the redox potential, E0(Cr6+/Cr3+) = 0.94 V and E0(•CO2 −/CO2) = −1.9 V. carbon centred free radicals can also reduce Cr(VI) at acidic condition.
(12) 3•CO2 − sodium alginate + Cr(VI) → Cr(III)- sodium alginate + 3CO2

On the other hand, As(III) ions could also be oxidized by Fe(III)- sodium alginate by the following way:
(13) Fe(III)- sodium alginate + As(III) + hv → 2Fe(II)- sodium alginate + As(V)

A photocatalytic paper is prepared by adding TiO2/sodium alginate nanocomposite with appropriate proportions (Rehim et al., 2016). The 7% and 15% TiO2/sodium alginate loaded photocatalytic paper show greater chemical oxygen demand removal efficiency. The reasons for the observed result are attributed to adhesion of nanoparticles to paper and reduced the harmful effect of the TiO2 through modification of sodium alginate. Also, alginate/TiO2, a buoyant photocatalyst has been reported for removal of tartrazine dye under ultraviolet illumination (Dalponte et al., 2019). Due to greater radiance utilization efficiency, this floatable photocatalyst showed 89% tartrazine discolouration efficiency. Moreover, the easier post-treatment separation of photocatalyst with no mechanical stirring is an added advantage. Apart from binary composite, alginate based ternary and quaternary composites have also been reported. For example, Majidnia et al. (Majidnia and Fulazzaky, 2016) have reported TiO2-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate beads and γ-Fe2O3-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate ternary composites for photo reduction of Pb(II) ions in water. γ-Fe2O3-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate ternary composite removes 98.3% of Pb(II) ions while TiO2-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate removes 98% of Pb(II) ions in water after 135 min of sunlight illumination.
(14) Pb2+ + 2e− → Pb
(15) H2O + 2h+ → 1/2O2 + 2H+
(16) Pb2+ + H2O + hυ → Pb + 1/2O2 + 2H+

Though the Pb(II) ions photoreduction efficiency of γ-Fe2O3-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate is higher, the TiO2-poly(vinyl alcohol)-alginate composite is proposed as best photocatalyst for Pb(II) ions removal from aqueous solution due to its high recycling efficiency. Huang et al. (2021) have reported alginate based quaternary photocatalytic recently. They have prepared BiOCl-g–C3N4–Fe3O4-alginate photocatalyst for photodegradation of methyl orange under visible light from light-emitting diodes. This photocatalyst shows 93.8% of degradation efficiency with rate constant value of 0.2087 h−1.

Pectin is a polysaccharide with esterified polygalacturonic acid. It exists in all higher plant cell wall (Broxterman and Schols, 2018). The pectin – CuS nanocomposite was synthesized by direct encapsulation of synthesized CuS into pectin (Gupta et al., 2012). Owing to the abundant COO− and OH− groups, pectin shows greater adsorption of methylene blue. During simultaneous adsorption and photocatalytic reaction assisted by sunlight illumination, 97% of methylene blue dye could be removed by pectin-CuS composite while pure CuS exhibits 70% degradation efficiency. Later, the same research group (Gupta et al., 2015) has synthesized pectin/zirconium(IV) selenotungstophosphate nanocomposite photocatalyst for decomposition of methylene blue and malachite green. Within 3 h of sunlight exposure, this composite shows 89.2% and 79.3% of photocatalytic degradation efficiency towards degradation of methylene blue and malachite green respectively. Recently, pectin-graphene oxide nanocomposite was prepared by Kaushal et al. (2020). About 20 mg of this composite photocatalyst is used for photo-oxidation of 50 mL (3.2 mg L−1) of methylene blue and methyl orange dyes under ultraviolet irradiation. In presence of pectin-graphene oxide nanocomposite, 98% of methylene blue and 87.5% of methyl orange is photo oxidized within 25 and 90 min of light exposure, respectively.

Collagen is a natural fibrous protein which contains hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2) carboxyl (-COOH) and amide (-CONH2) functional groups (Ahmed et al., 2020b). It is abundantly present in skin, ligaments, tendons, bones and connective tissues in mammals. Owing to its hierarchical structure, biodegradability and biocompatibility, it is used in preparation of composite photocatalysts. For instance, different weight percentile of modified cationic collagen molecules were immobilized on Fe3+ doped TiO2 nanostructures which have been used as novel photocatalysts for decomposition of methyl orange in under solar illumination (Li et al., 2015). In particular, 3 wt% modified collagen loaded Fe3+ doped TiO2 nanoparticles show maximal degradation efficiency (~96%) with a much faster reaction rate. The modified collagen has –N(CH3)3 +, hence its surface immobilization on Fe3+ doped TiO2 particle's surface significantly promotes rapid migration of negatively charged methyl orange molecules from bulk solution into Fe3+ doped TiO2 via strong electrostatic attractions and establish the adsorption equilibrium. The surface adsorbed dye molecule undergoes degradation at photocatalyst surface rapidly. In addition, adsorption sites could also act as trap centres for photoinduced electrons. Recently, Zhou et al. (2018) have prepared silver salts such as silver acetate (CH3COOAg), silver chloride (AgCl), silver phosphate (Ag3PO4), silver molybdate (Ag2MoO4) and silver tungstate (Ag2WO4) loaded collagen nanohybrids by in-situ generation method. Their photocatalytic performances were determined by using methyl orange as model organic pollutant. The ultraviolet light and visible light sources have been used for irradiation purpose. The order of photocatalytic performance under visible light illumination for 35 min is shown below: AgCl/collagen > Ag2MoO4/collagen > Ag3PO4/collagen ≈ CH3COOAg/collagen > Ag2WO4/collagen > collagen. The same trend is observed for under ultraviolet irradiation of 210 min. Among other nanohybrids, AgCl/collagen hybrid exhibited best photocatalytic performance under ultraviolet and visible light illumination. Besides, AgCl (~3.3 eV) is not a visible active photocatalyst, but it shows activity under visible light irradiation. Authors of this research work have explained the observed activity of AgCl/collagen by the following way: Visible light irradiation can cause partial decomposition of AgCl and form Ag nanoparticles. Due to surface plasmon resonance effect, Ag nanoparticles absorb visible light and generate electron and hole pairs. The plasmon-induced electron is shifted to the conduction band of AgCl and reacts with oxygen to form superoxide anionic radicals. While holes in the valence band of Ag nanoparticles is migrated to the surface of AgCl and react with chloride ions, leading to the formation of chlorine (Cl0) atoms. Both superoxide anionic radicals and chlorine atoms are powerful oxidants and thus they decompose methyl orange dye molecules effectively.
(17) AgCl + hν (visible) → Ag0 + Cl−
(18) (Ag)h+ + AgCl → Ag+ + Cl0

For the past decade, number for green synthesized photocatalysts and biopolymer based composite photocatalysts has been reported. In the majority of these publications, the reported photocatalysts show advantages such as visible light harvesting capacity, high photocatalytic efficiency, and stability in laboratory scale studies. Nevertheless, their performances in waste water treatment plants are unclear. Furthermore, the problem of recovery and recycling photocatalysts has not been addressed completely. The toxicity of the by-products/intermediates from photocatalytic degradation reaction to living organisms is also limitedly available. The optimized reaction parameters for green synthesis for photocatalysts need to be studied in detail. Green synthetic approaches for novel composite photocatalyst need to be developed. The fate of biopolymers after photocatalytic degradations reactions is not well studied. There remain significant challenges for practical utilization in water treatment plants.

This review summarizes recent progress of biosynthesized metals, metal oxides, metal compounds, carbon materials and their nanocomposites and biopolymer based photocatalysts for the removal of organic, inorganic and organometallic aquatic pollutants. Different types of photocatalytic mechanism of binary composites, biosynthesis method for nanomaterial production and widely studied biopolymers are discussed providing better understandings. The summary and conclusive points were drawn from this review:
i.
By using green synthesis method, pure metal oxide, metals, carbon materials and mixed metal oxide nanocomposites have been prepared.
ii.
Green synthesized photocatalytic materials show greater photocatalytic response owing to their narrow band gap, greater adsorption capacity and porosity.
iii.
Green synthesized photocatalysts are showing good antimicrobial property which is an added advantage in waste water treatment.
iv.
Drawbacks of green synthesized photocatalysts are as follows: (a) practical difficulties in recovery of the photocatalyst during their synthesis, (b) limitation of light penetration due to its absorption by surface adsorbed organic contents on photocatalyst, (c) opacity of the photocatalyst slurry, (d) requirement of large biomass quantities.
v.
Biopolymers such as chitin, chitosan, pectin, hydroxyapatite, starch, and collagen based composite photocatalysts were developed due to the following reasons: (a) high functionality, (b) reactivity, (c) large surface area, (d) antimicrobial activity, (e) biocompatibility, (f) ease of processing and brings stability to photocatalytic particles (g) act as electron trapping centres (h) produce electron hole pairs.
vi.
Research efforts need to be given on the preparation of sustainable photocatalysts that have been prepared from wastes.

R. Suresh, Saravanan Rajendran: Writing- Original draft preparation. Lorena Cornejo-Ponce, Dai-Viet N. Vo: Conceptualization, Methodology and discussion. Saravanan Rajendran, Tuan K.A. Hoang: Reviewing and editing.
